stem: A hospital uses an ELISA screening test to screen for Lyme disease. The test has a sensitivity of 93% and a specificity of 93%. The prevalence of Lyme disease in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.8% and 4.3%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has Lyme disease? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(Lyme disease | positive test) ranges from 0.196 to 0.374 as prevalence varies from 1.8% to 4.3%. Point estimate at 3.0% prevalence: 0.291.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.07.
The confidence interval [0.196, 0.374] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (93%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
